items.title: Kaltrinkeda — Home Screen V2
items.author: Kirey Cazkdnsky
items.content:
Esse é o novíssimo design experimental da Home Screen do Kaltrinkeda, nossa IDE tem um grande diferencial, pois seu design é 100% baseado em Blur, ela não tem tema claro, mas planejo tornar as cores Customizáveis

Nossa IDE tem algo único, um background Animado na home screen, esse design remete a Poeira, partículas flutuando pela tela, e tudo isso sem explodir seu dispositivo

Essa animação é muito bem otimizada, pois nada é pré-definido, as partículas de poeira se movem aleatoriamente pela tela usando Cálculos Matemáticos, ou seja, um script de 5 linhas vai pegar números de 1 a 50 aleatoriamente e fazer eles se moverem pra algum lugar aleatório

Além de que as partículas não são renderizadas em HD, na verdade elas são meros Agrupamentos de Pixels, embora pra você isso pareça uma Esfera, na verdade é em média 1 a 10 pixels unidos com efeito de Blur sobre eles, oque faz parecer que é redondo, o blur das partículas são calculadas ao mesmo tempo, ao invés de cada partícula ter um Blur independente, é 1 único efeito que está sobre todas ao mesmo tempo

As partículas além de se moverem elas piscam, e isso é Detalhe.

A Home Screen tem 3 Containers principais;

Welcome Back: Onde você encontra os botões de criar Projeto, File, gerenciar Backups, conhecer funções novas, dar Suporte ao projeto, e no geral a Interação do usuário, esses botões são essenciais.

Projetos Recentes: Um histórico do oque você faz no app, o plano é que esse histórico consiga salvar seu Progresso, para assim você poder acessar versões antigas do seu código, ou seja, se o código Funcionava mas você fez algo e parou de funcionar, você poderia Voltar nessa versão que funcionava, e Comparar elas pra ver oque deu errado. A idéia é que isso ocorra de forma Nativa, com o app exibindo em highlight todas as Mudanças e até Possíveis Erros e Problemas.

Documents: Nessa área ficam seus documentos, Arquivos, Projetos, TXTs, Notas e mais. O inovador disso é a Estilização, cada file tem uma cor Diferente baseado na linguagem dele, e o embed muda de cor baseado nisso, arquivos Python são Azuis, os JS são Amarelos, Projetos não tem cor, Java é Vermelho, Ruby é Laranja e por ai vai.

A aba Documents não apenas mostra o arquivo, ela exibe Peso, Nome, Linguagem, Oque É, Bibliotecas que esse File/Projeto usa, no futuro, será possível Baixar os arquivos, fazer Backups e muito mais

Não há novidades sobre as Configurações do app, porquê quero que você se surpreenda, então vou guardar isso pra um futuro post, mas posso dizer que

Haverá customização, desde que ela não torne o app pesado, porquê o intuito da NMBAH é o oposto disso, é te entregar um app Leve, Otimizado e Funcional

Você poderá até mesmo Desativar o background se por acaso seu dispositivo estiver travando, e toda nossa otimização falhe, porquê as vezes a pessoa tem um dispositivo e ruim e pouca condição

A pessoa pode ser Pobre, e o dispositivo dela é a única coisa que ela pode ter por enquanto, então se isso torna o app pesado, não podemos implementar.

O foco aqui não é Customização, e sim Funcionalidade, a prioridade é que o app funcione, que ele não tenha Bugs, e que esteja bem Otimizado.

Além de que sim, nosso Blur é otimizado

Usamos algo chamado Blur Onion, que é basicamente Camadas, o usuário enxerga o Blur, mas abaixo dele tem uma camada de Mosaico Pixelado, e isso força o Blur a processar uma imagem Pixelada, dessa forma, ele usa Menos Processamento, Carrega Mais Rápido, e não precisa Calcular Tanto, já que a quantia de pixels é reduzida, o processamento é mais rápido e eficiente.

A NMBAH não investe em lucro, ela investe em uma Experiência Inesquecível

E é isso que nosso app deve fazer, o usuário deve Entrar, mesmo sem nunca ter visto o app, saber mexer nele sem menor dificuldade, uma interface fácil, intuitiva

Nós te damos de graça oque IDEs famosas cobram

E te damos muito mais coisa que elas, de graça, incluindo oque elas oferecem, e muitos adicionais.

items.title: 🎯Fermat’s Little Theorem: The Tiny Theorem with Huge Impact
items.author: Rithika R
items.content:
says:

If ( p ) is a prime number, and ( a ) is an integer , then:

✅ This means when ( a^{p-1} ) is divided by ( p ), the .

🔁 An alternate, form of the theorem:

💡 Why it matters: This second version even works when ( a ) divisible by ( p ), because both sides will be congruent to 0 mod ( p ).

🧱 2. Understanding the Components

Here’s what the terms mean:

  • e.g. 2, 3, 5, 7, 11…

    Only divisible by 1 and itself.

  • Any whole number, positive, negative, or 0.

  • ( a \not\equiv 0 \mod p )

    ( a ) is by ( p ). The remainder isn’t zero.

  • Congruence notation.

    Means both sides leave the same remainder when divided by ( p ).

🔢 3. 

3^(5-1) ≡ 1  (mod 5)
3^4 = 81
81 ≡ 1  (mod 5)

✔️ Since ( 81 - 1 = 80 ) is divisible by 5, the theorem holds.

2^(7-1) ≡ 1  (mod 7)
2^6 = 64
64 ≡ 1  (mod 7)

✔️ ( 64 - 1 = 63 ), which is 7 × 9.

Let ( p = 3 ), ( a = 6 ) (divisible by 3)

6^3 ≡ 6  (mod 3)
216 ≡ 6  (mod 3)

✔️ Both 216 and 6 leave when divided by 3.

So even though ( a ) is divisible by ( p ), the congruence still holds.

Fermat's Little Theorem is more than a neat math trick—it’s a workhorse in:

Pick an integer ( a ) and test:

  • ❌ If false → ( n ) is definitely .
  • ✅ If true for many values of ( a ), ( n ) might be prime.

🧠 Used in fast primality tests like , , etc.

Example: RSA, Diffie-Hellman

  • 🔁 Helps find : 
  a^(p-2) ≡ a^(-1)  (mod p)
  • 🧠 Verifies RSA using exponentiation tricks

Fermat's Little Theorem helps ensure your encrypted messages are safe.

⚙️ Efficient Modular Exponentiation

Rather than computing massive powers directly:

a^k mod p → a^(k % (p-1)) mod p

🧠 Reduce the exponent using Fermat's Theorem and simplify big calculations.

🤯 3^17 is HUGE… but using Fermat:

  • Since 5 is prime → ( 3^4 ≡ 1 \mod 5 )

🙌 Answer in seconds instead of minutes.

  • Used in for RSA decryption
  • Primality tests for
  • Ensures of algorithms

    Reduces time and resources in:

    • Modular arithmetic-heavy software
    ( a^{p-1} ≡ 1 \mod p ) (if ( p ) prime, ( a \not\equiv 0 \mod p ))
    Foundation of cryptography and modular arithmetic
    RSA, primality testing, key exchange, efficient computation

    🧮 8. Conclusion: A Small Theorem with a Giant Legacy

    Fermat’s Little Theorem may be in name, but its power is massive.

    From primality testing to cryptographic security, this centuries-old insight fuels much of the today. Whether you're building a blockchain, securing messages, or optimizing algorithms, you're likely standing on the shoulders of this tiny theorem.

items.title: Top 10 YouTube Channels for Learning Data Analytics: Boost Your Skills in 2025
items.author: Vlada Maksymiuk
items.content:
Thousands of educational channels on YouTube cover a variety of subjects, with data analytics being one of the most popular topics. But with so many options out there, how do you decide which channels are worth your time? What videos should you watch to stay up-to-date with industry trends and level up your skills?

In this post, I’ve curated a list of the top YouTube channels for data analytics, complete with a quick rundown of what each offers and which videos you should check out.

Why Data Analytics Is Essential in Today’s World The demand for skilled data professionals is growing rapidly. As organizations continue to rely on data-driven decisions, roles like data analysts and scientists are in high demand. According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, data science employment is expected to increase by a staggering 36% between 2021 and 2031. If you want to get into this field, now is the perfect time to start!

Why YouTube Is a Game-Changer for Learning Data Analytics Looking to become a data analyst or advance your career? YouTube can be a great resource. Videos break down complex topics in a digestible way, often accompanied by visuals and real-world examples. Plus, many creators share the latest trends and best practices in data analytics.

With 2.49 billion monthly users (according to Semrush), YouTube isn’t just for watching cat videos anymore. It’s a thriving learning platform, offering:

  • Accessibility: You can watch videos from anywhere, on any device, as long as you have an internet connection.
  • Availability: Learning is free! Most videos are accessible at no cost, making it an excellent resource for self-starters.
  • Diversity: Over 500 hours of content are uploaded every minute on YouTube. No matter what aspect of data analytics you're interested in, there’s likely a video on it.
  • Learning at Your Own Pace: You can pause, replay, or speed up the videos — a personalized experience that lets you learn on your own terms.
  • Live Streaming: Many instructors offer live sessions, webinars, and online lessons, creating opportunities for real-time learning.
  • Engagement: The comments section on each video is a great place to ask questions, join discussions, and connect with other learners and professionals.

Here, you’ll find my handpicked list of the best YouTube channels to learn data analytics. Whether you're just starting or looking to dive deeper into the subject, these channels offer a variety of high-quality content that can help you expand your knowledge.

Top YouTube Channels for Data Analytics

Simplilearn is one of the largest online boot camps, with a presence in San Francisco and Bangalore. The platform offers comprehensive training in a range of tech fields, including data analytics, digital marketing, cloud architecture, and more. Their YouTube channel is home to thousands of videos covering a variety of topics, explained in simple terms, making it an excellent resource for beginners in data analytics.

  • Accessible for beginners.
  • Covers broad data-related topics.
  • Offers detailed practical exercises and exams.
  • Primarily focuses on broad overviews; less in-depth for advanced learners.

Alex Freberg, the creator behind Alex The Analyst, brings over 10 years of hands-on experience in data analytics. His YouTube channel offers straightforward tutorials aimed at both beginners and intermediate learners. He covers essential tools like Excel, Power BI, SQL, and Python, offering practical tips and real-life examples that make the learning process enjoyable and engaging.

  • Focuses on hands-on tutorials and real-world applications.
  • Covers essential tools like Power BI and SQL.
  • Excellent for those interested in practical tips and workflows.
  • Aimed more at entry-level to intermediate learners, might lack content for experts.

Data School is an online resource dedicated to advancing the knowledge of data science and analytics. It offers a mix of detailed courses, blog posts, and YouTube videos designed to help aspiring data professionals. Whether you're starting out or looking to level up your skills, Data School's YouTube channel provides valuable insights into data science topics, explained in a straightforward way.

  • Detailed, hands-on tutorials.
  • Focuses on real-world applications in data science.
  • Ideal for analysts looking to advance to data science.
  • More suited for learners with basic knowledge in data analysis.
  • Limited content for beginners who are new to data science.

365 Data Science is a comprehensive online platform offering a wide range of courses and certifications in data science and analytics. It caters to those looking to start a career in data, as well as professionals seeking to advance or update their knowledge. The YouTube channel provides insights on various data-related careers and offers themed playlists to help users choose their data path.

  • Extensive range of data science and analytics content.
  • Career-specific playlists help navigate professional paths.
  • Highly regarded for its structured learning approach.
  • Content may be overwhelming for absolute beginners.
  • Focuses more on career progression rather than niche topics.

Ken Jee is a data scientist who specializes in artificial intelligence and sports analytics. His YouTube channel blends fun, informative content with practical advice, helping both budding data scientists and professionals understand complex concepts. Additionally, Ken covers news and trends in the data field, offering valuable insights into the industry's evolution.

  • Engaging and approachable style.
  • Covers practical career advice and industry trends.
  • Focuses on real-life applications of data science.
  • Content may be more suited to intermediate learners rather than absolute beginners.
  • Less focused on in-depth technical tutorials.

DataCamp is a renowned learning platform that offers courses in data analysis, machine learning, AI, data engineering, and more. Its YouTube channel provides a mix of educational videos and industry insights, featuring best practices from companies and trends in the data field. DataCamp’s channel is perfect for those looking to stay up-to-date with data industry developments.

  • Trusted platform with a solid reputation in data science education.
  • High-quality tutorials and trend-based content.
  • Great resource for industry professionals looking to stay current.
  • Many videos focus on trends rather than deep dives into specific topics.
  • Channel is more geared towards a general audience rather than niche areas.

Skyvia is a versatile data platform designed to simplify a wide range of data-related tasks, from workflow automation and data integration to backup and OData endpoint creation. It’s an ideal solution for data analysts as it helps streamline processes from data collection to loading data into BI and analytics tools.

Skyvia's YouTube channel offers a wealth of educational content, including webinars, tutorials, and data integration scenarios. While not entirely focused on data analytics, the channel provides valuable resources that can help data professionals get the most out of Skyvia's functionalities.

  • Wide range of data integration and automation features.
  • Educational content, including tutorials and webinars.
  • User-friendly interface suitable for data analysts.
  • Not solely focused on data analytics.
  • Limited in-depth analytics-specific resources.

8. StatQuest with Josh Starmer

StatQuest is an essential resource for anyone diving into the world of data science and analytics. Managed by Josh Starmer, the channel simplifies complex statistical formulas and concepts, particularly in the context of machine learning algorithms. Josh’s clear and engaging style has made him a top YouTube educator in the space.

  • Clear, digestible explanations of complex statistics and machine learning concepts.
  • Great for beginners and those looking to solidify their understanding of foundational statistics.
  • Engaging and entertaining presentation style.
  • Focuses more on statistics and foundational concepts rather than advanced data analytics.
  • Some topics might be too basic for advanced learners.

FreeCodeCamp.org is a trusted platform that offers free coding courses, ranging from basic HTML to Python for data analysis. While data analysts don't necessarily need to be professional developers, knowledge of coding is often required. FreeCodeCamp’s YouTube channel provides a comprehensive array of tutorials for aspiring data professionals.

  • A wide range of tutorials on various programming languages and tools.
  • Free, high-quality educational content suitable for all levels.
  • Focuses on both coding skills and data-specific tools.
  • Content may be overwhelming for beginners as it covers a broad spectrum of topics.
  • Less focused on specialized data analytics tools or techniques.

Power BI, a leading tool in the BI and data analytics space, is widely recognized for its Microsoft interface and strong integration with other Microsoft products. It's ideal for data professionals without coding experience and is popular for its mobile app, making it highly accessible.

The Power BI YouTube channel offers insights into recent updates, detailed feature breakdowns, and tutorials to help users get the most out of this powerful tool.

  • Great for both beginners and advanced users.
  • Strong integration with Microsoft products.
  • Frequent updates and helpful feature tutorials.
  • Limited to Power BI users, not covering broader data analytics topics.
  • Some advanced functionalities may require additional training.

How to Choose the Right Channel

Selecting the right YouTube channel for your data analytics journey depends on your career goals and background. Here’s a quick guide:

  • For Data Analysts: If you’re starting your career, , , and are excellent for building a solid foundation.
  • For Aspiring Data Scientists: If you want to transition from data analyst to data scientist, check out , , and .
  • For Programming: For programming skills, is your go-to channel.
  • For Stats and Machine Learning: StatQuest with Josh Starmer is perfect for mastering statistical concepts.
  • For Data Tools: Explore and to dive into data preparation and analysis tools.

YouTube is a great starting point for learning data analytics at your own pace, but complement it with books, courses, and hands-on experience to truly excel. What other channels or resources have helped you on your data journey? Share in the comments!

items.title: The Impact of 5G on Web Development: How Faster Networks Will Change User Experiences
items.author: Rushil Bhuptani
items.content:
The digital experience is going to reach warp speed. The scene for online and mobile app development is changing at an unmatched speed as 5G networks spread throughout the world.

But what does this imply for companies developing websites? Faster networks will alter design concepts, backend logic, and user expectations in what way?

Let us analyze what 5G actually is and how professional web development firms may be ready for the new digital normal.

What Is 5G and Why Does It Matter for Web Development?

5G (fifth-generation wireless technology) offers:

  • Up to 100x faster data transfer speeds than 4G
  • Lower latency (as low as 1ms)
  • Support for more connected devices
  • Higher network reliability and bandwidth

Essentially: reduced buffering, more real-time responsiveness, and improved support for immersive, media-heavy experiences.

A web development company using 5G-ready techniques may provide quicker, more seamless, and more user-friendly apps, vital in the competitive environment of today.

How 5G Will Transform User Experience Online

Faster networks aren't just a tech upgrade—they're a UX revolution. Here's how:

🎯 1. Real-Time Experiences Become Seamless 5G enables real-time updates, live interactions, and instant responsiveness across all platforms. Chat apps, live auctions, multiplayer games, and real-time dashboards will feel truly “live.”

🎮 2. Richer Multimedia Without Lag With increased bandwidth, high-res images, 4K/8K video, and 3D assets load faster. This allows front-end web development companies to push the creative envelope without performance trade-offs.

📱 3. Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) Thrive PWAs will perform closer to native apps, with less reliance on caching or preloading tricks. 5G removes many of the constraints that previously hindered browser-based experiences.

🌍 4. Edge Computing & IoT Support With edge processing and low-latency networks, web apps can interact with IoT devices in real time, useful for logistics, healthcare, and smart city applications.

🧠 5. AI/AR Integration at Scale Advanced tech like augmented reality and AI-powered interactions is no longer limited by bandwidth. A custom web development company can now craft intelligent, immersive interfaces powered by real-time data.

Implications for Web Developers and Companies

Faster networks shift the way we approach development. Here's what developers and companies need to keep in mind:

🚀 Heavier Frontends = No Excuse for Sluggish Sites Yes, 5G allows heavier frontends—but it doesn't excuse poor optimization. Users will expect near-instant load times and fluid interactions. Web development services must double down on front-end performance best practices.

🔄 Real-Time Frameworks Will Become Standard Expect an uptick in the use of technologies like WebSockets, GraphQL Subscriptions, and server-sent events to power real-time functionality.

🛠️ New Tools and Languages in the Stack From Flutter Web to Blazor (for C# web development), modern frameworks will shine under 5G. The emphasis will be on fast-rendering, reactive components.

🔐 Greater Need for Robust Backend Security More interactivity means more attack surfaces. As 5G amplifies web capabilities, secure backend practices and data protection become more critical.

🌐 Cross-Device Compatibility Will Matter More Than Ever A reliable, fast experience across phones, tablets, and wearables will become the standard expectation. That’s where a professional web development company with mobile-first thinking can stand out.

Opportunities for Web Development Companies in the 5G Era

This is a moment of disruption—and opportunity. Forward-thinking web and mobile app development companies can:

🧩 Offer Next-Gen App Experiences Build apps with AR, real-time location tracking, or video-heavy content that was previously limited by mobile data speed.

🎯 Target Emerging Industries Telemedicine, smart cities, autonomous vehicles, and EdTech platforms will require robust 5G-compatible web applications.

🧑‍💻 Offer 5G Optimization Services Help clients refactor legacy systems and web apps for 5G performance expectations.

🎥 Create Streaming-Friendly Platforms Media, entertainment, and social networking brands need high-quality, lag-free platforms for video and audio.

Whether it’s building a dynamic single-page app (SPA), a WebRTC-powered conferencing tool, or a sensor-connected web portal, 5G unlocks vast possibilities for custom web development companies.

5G and Web App Performance: Key Considerations

Even with 5G’s superpowers, good performance starts at the code level.

  • Optimizing images and media
  • Compressing scripts and stylesheets
  • Leveraging service workers and caching
  • Using CDNs and edge computing

5G gives you the bandwidth, but performance is still in the developer’s hands. That’s where choosing the right web application development company makes all the difference.

Final Thoughts: 5G Is the Catalyst—Now It’s Up to Us

As 5G adoption grows, users will expect faster, smarter, and more immersive digital experiences by default. The margin for latency, clunky design, or outdated architecture will shrink dramatically.

The best web development companies will embrace this shift, offering innovative, future-ready web development services that go beyond just mobile responsiveness or SEO checklists.

If your team is planning a platform refresh or thinking about launching a media-rich web app, now is the time to future-proof it for 5G.

items.title: Beyond If-Else Hell: Elegant State Machine pattern in Game Development
items.author: Niraj Vishwakarma
items.content:
The State pattern is a behavioral design pattern that allows an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes. This pattern is particularly useful when an object's behavior depends on its state and must change dynamically at runtime.

State can be defined as the current condition of an object or system, i.e., the state decides the behaviour of an object or system. For example:

  • Morning, Day, Afternoon, Evening, Night
  • Sunny, Snowing, Raining
  • Character physical states: Idle, Walking, Sleeping, Chasing, Hiding, Healthy, Hurt

A is a behavioral model that comprises a finite number of states, hence it is also referred to as a finite state machine (FSM). It operates based on the current state and a specified input, facilitating state transitions, and generating corresponding outputs.

When an input is received, the state machine transitions from its current state to a new state based on predefined rules or transitions defined by the system's design. These transitions dictate how the system responds to different inputs or conditions.

Let us take an example of a and break down its behaviour into a state machine:

Another interesting example can be the state of an or agent in a game scenario. The agent's behavior may change based on whether it can see the player. In such a situation, a state machine can manage the agent's states and transitions, allowing it to focus on specific behaviors within each state.

Following are the states of the AI bot:

  • : Initial state where the AI bot is not actively engaged in any action.
  • : The AI bot is walking around the environment.
  • : The AI bot is actively chasing the player once they are in visible range.
  • : The AI bot is being attacked by the player.

Following are the state transitions of an AI bot:

  • : Transition to a walking state when the AI bot starts patrolling or moving around.
  • : Transition to chasing state when the player becomes visible within the AI bot's range.
  • : Transition to patrol state when the player goes out of sight of the AI Bot.

By defining these key components for each state class, the overall behavior of the state machine can be clearly delineated, facilitating modular design and maintainability. Each state encapsulates its specific behavior, allowing for easier comprehension, modification, and extension of the system's functionality.

Next, we will look at how we can implement a state machine for a given problem. We want to implement a state machine to control a character controller with various states such as attacking, defending, and returning home.

Implementing state machine using inheritance

The steps to be followed for implementing state machine using inheritance are as follows:

  1. Define a base state class.
  2. Create Separate classes for each concrete state, inheriting the base state class.
  3. State Machine will manage transition among different states. 
// Creating base state

public abstract class CharacterState
{
    public abstract void EnterState(CharacterController controller);
    public abstract void Update(CharacterController controller);
    public abstract void ExitState(CharacterController controller);
}


// Creating Concrete states inheriting Base State

public class AttackingState : CharacterState
{
    public override void EnterState(CharacterController controller)
    {
        // Enter Attacking state logic
    }

    public override void Update(CharacterController controller)
    {
        // Attacking state logic
    }

    public override void ExitState(CharacterController controller)
    {
        // Exit Attacking state logic
    }
}

Similarly, other states like can be implemented. Next, we will implement a in which state transition logic will be implemented, as shown:

// Character Controller class

public class CharacterController
{
    private CharacterState currentState;

    public void ChangeState(CharacterState newState)
    {
        if (currentState != null)
        {
            currentState.ExitState(this);
        }

        currentState = newState;
        currentState.EnterState(this);
    }

    public void Update()
    {
        if (currentState != null)
        {
            currentState.Update(this);
        }
    }
}

The class manages the current state and transitions between states based on external events or conditions. This approach allows for flexible and modular design, making it easier to add, remove, or modify states as needed.

Game design patterns and their usage are explained in the the context of game development with great details in the book: Learning Game Architecture with Unity, which is available @ Amazon, Amazon India, BPB International, BPB India.

items.title: PM2 Integration with Jenkins on Windows — Observations and Alternative Recommendations
items.author: BAKRE JAMIU
items.content:
Hit a wall trying to run PM2 within Jenkins on a Windows machine 😅 After testing PM2, Forever, NSSM, and Task Scheduler, it’s clear that Windows isn’t PM2’s playground. Sharing this to help anyone facing the same CI/CD challenge 💡

During my recent CI/CD pipeline implementation using Jenkins on a Windows environment, I attempted to integrate as a background process manager for a Node.js application. Despite successfully installing PM2 and validating it via CLI, I encountered issues running it persistently within the Jenkins pipeline.

The process did not behave as expected — PM2 failed to run in the background through Jenkins, and the functionality was no different than invoking directly. Extensive troubleshooting, including using , global installs, and different execution flags (, etc.), did not resolve the issue.

I also explored potential Jenkins plugins for PM2 integration but confirmed that no native or community-supported plugins are currently available.

As a result, I evaluated alternative Node.js process managers that support Windows environments:

  • – lightweight but lacks advanced features like monitoring and clustering.
  • NSSM (Non-Sucking Service Manager) – effective for converting scripts to Windows services, though limited in logs and ecosystem integration.
  • – native but lacks real-time monitoring and isn't CI/CD friendly.

Unfortunately, none of the alternatives offer the robustness and features of PM2, particularly in CI/CD pipelines running on Windows.

I’m sharing this update for awareness and future reference. If there’s a more effective service/process manager that supports background execution for Node.js apps within Jenkins on Windows, I’m open to exploring it and sharing further findings.

Thanks,

items.title: How to Learn Backend Development: A Beginner’s Roadmap to Master the Web’s Powerhouse
items.author: Tarun
items.content:
When I first dipped my toes into web development, I was all about making things look good. Frontend was shiny, immediate, and gratifying—click a button, and it does something cool. But as I started building more complex projects, I hit a wall. I realized all the magic wasn’t just happening on the surface. It was what’s behind the scenes—the backend—that gave life to the application. That’s when I decided to flip the coin and dive into the world of backend development.

And if you’re reading this, chances are you're at that point too. You want to go beyond the visuals and understand how websites actually work. So, let me take you on a journey—not just a tutorial, but a guide with real steps, resources, and insights to become a backend developer.

What is Backend Development?

Before you jump into the how, let’s understand the what.

Backend development refers to the part of web development that focuses on what users don’t see. It’s about how data is stored, how servers work, how APIs are designed, and how everything comes together to respond to what the user does on the frontend.

Think of it like a restaurant. The frontend is the waiter taking your order, and the backend is the kitchen where the actual cooking happens. If the kitchen doesn’t work well, your dining experience will be a disaster—no matter how charming the waiter is.

How to Learn Backend Development?

So, what exactly do backend developers need to learn? Here’s a roadmap to get you started:

Start by mastering one backend programming language. The most popular ones are:

  • Node.js (JavaScript) – great for full-stack devs who already know frontend JS.
  • Python – clean, readable, and beginner-friendly. Often used with frameworks like Django or Flask.
  • PHP – still widely used, especially in WordPress-based projects.
  • Java – robust and enterprise-ready.
  • Ruby – used with Ruby on Rails for rapid development.

🧠 Tip: Start with Python or Node.js if you’re a complete beginner. They have huge communities and tons of beginner resources.

2. Understanding Databases

Data is the heart of any application, and as a backend developer, you’ll be dealing with it a lot.

  1. SQL Databases: MySQL, PostgreSQL
  2. NoSQL Databases: MongoDB, Firebase
  • Create, read, update, and delete data (CRUD operations)
  • Design schemas and relationships between tables
  • Optimize queries for performance

3. APIs: The Bridge Between Front and Back

APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) are how the frontend talks to the backend.

    GraphQL APIs (for more advanced usage)

    • HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
    • Authentication (JWT, OAuth)

    4. Authentication and Security

    Security is not optional in backend development. Learn:

    • How to implement login systems
    • How to protect against common vulnerabilities (SQL injection, XSS, CSRF)

    5. Version Control with Git

    Backend projects can quickly become massive. Learning Git will help you:

    • Track changes in your code
    • Collaborate with others via platforms like GitHub or GitLab
    • Roll back to previous versions if something breaks

    Frameworks make life easier by handling repetitive tasks.

    Popular backend frameworks:

      Pick one based on the language you choose and stick with it till you're confident.

      Real Projects: The Secret to Fast Learning

      Reading and tutorials are great, but nothing beats building real projects. Start small:

      A blog site with login/signup

      A to-do list app with a database

      A notes app with REST API functionality

      Then level up to more complex ideas: E-commerce backend with product and cart management

      Social media backend with user posts, likes, and comments

      A job board with authentication and filtering

      🚀 Pro Tip: Push your code to GitHub. It builds your portfolio and helps you stay consistent.

      Learn by Doing: My Personal Story

      When I started learning backend, I made a simple REST API for a book store. At first, I struggled with database schemas and got confused between PUT and PATCH requests. But I kept at it. I googled endlessly, watched YouTube videos late into the night, and broke my app more times than I can count.

      I deployed my app, added authentication, and shared it with my friends. That small victory made me feel like a real developer. And guess what? That confidence led me to land my first freelance project.

      So yes, backend can seem intimidating. But take it step by step, and you’ll get there.

      Frontend may get all the applause, but backend is the brain of the application. It’s what gives your app memory, intelligence, and the ability to grow.

      So, if you're ready to dig deeper, embrace a bit of complexity, and build the invisible engines that power the internet—backend development is your calling.

      Start today, and a year from now, you’ll look back and smile at how far you’ve come.

items.title: RoboRescue Puzzle . A logic-based grid navigation game
items.author: Akua Konadu
items.content:
RoboRescue Puzzle is a simple robot-themed puzzle game. Players guide a robot through a 5x5 grid filled with obstacles, aiming to reach the green goal tile. Players use commands like “move forward” or “turn” to solve each level. The game has multiple levels, a level select menu, a clean robot-style interface, and saves progress using both local storage and Alibaba Cloud.

Alibaba Cloud Services Implementation

: We needed a lightweight backend to collect and sync player progress (level, time, moves) from the client side without setting up a full backend server.

: We created a simple Function Compute endpoint that receives a POST request with gameplay data and stores it.

: After each level is completed, a fetch() POST request sends the player's current progress to our Function Compute endpoint.

: The deployment was fast and straightforward. Function Compute was ideal for serverless handling of progress data. The main challenge was setting up proper CORS headers and endpoint permissions, but Alibaba’s documentation helped resolve that.

Object Storage Service (OSS)

: OSS was used to host static assets like the robot image and optionally level configuration JSON files.

: Game assets such as robot.png are hosted on OSS and loaded directly into the game using their public URLs.

: The robot image is retrieved from OSS at runtime and rendered on the game board. This makes updating assets easier without needing to redeploy the entire app.

: Very smooth experience. We appreciated the high availability and CDN-backed delivery which ensured fast loading, even on mobile devices.

Game Development Highlights

items.title: Rogue Domain Controller Detection in Cloud Identity Infrastructure
items.author: Mikuz
items.content:
Introduction: The Expanding Threat of Identity System Exploits

As enterprises accelerate their migration to the cloud, attackers are adapting their methods to exploit virtual identity systems. One of the most dangerous tactics involves deploying hidden components that simulate legitimate infrastructure—such as a —to quietly intercept or manipulate authentication data.

Even in cloud environments without traditional on-prem domain controllers, identity services can still be compromised through virtual machines, misconfigured synchronization tools, or cloud-native directory integrations. This article focuses on identifying, isolating, and preventing these unauthorized infrastructure elements in modern cloud ecosystems.

1. Identity Infrastructure Risks in Virtualized Networks

In a cloud-first environment, organizations often assume infrastructure is fully secured by default. However, identity services—whether connected to Azure AD, AWS IAM, or third-party federation tools—are vulnerable to infrastructure-level impersonation and replication attacks.

Common Vulnerabilities Include:

  • Improperly segmented cloud VMs that host directory services
  • Excessively privileged identity synchronization tools (e.g., Azure AD Connect)
  • Outdated or misconfigured hybrid identity bridges
  • Weak administrative oversight over new machine identities

Without robust monitoring, attackers can establish unauthorized machines that behave like trusted components in the authentication chain.

2. Techniques Attackers Use to Deploy Rogue Infrastructure

Virtual Machine Deployment

Cloud environments often allow users to quickly deploy virtual machines. A compromised or misused account can create a machine with the appearance of a legitimate domain service node.

Exploiting Directory Synchronization Tools

If synchronization agents are not locked down, attackers can hijack sync traffic or impersonate trusted services using forged certificates or stolen tokens.

Abusing Administrative Privileges

Cloud roles with elevated privileges are a key target. Once compromised, they can be used to authorize or replicate infrastructure changes without triggering alerts.

3. Indicators of Unauthorized Infrastructure in the Cloud

Organizations should maintain visibility across both infrastructure and identity layers. Key signs of unauthorized elements in your cloud environment include:

  • Unexpected VM provisioning events linked to identity roles
  • Unauthorized replication behavior in hybrid sync logs
  • Sudden changes to machine metadata (e.g., instance tags or security groups)
  • Suspicious identity authentication patterns (e.g., service principals connecting from new IPs)
  • Domain controller-like behavior in non-domain environments

Tracking these indicators helps distinguish normal identity operations from malicious activity.

4. Detection Strategies Across Cloud Platforms

Log Correlation Across Identity Services

Use native logging tools like Azure Monitor, AWS CloudTrail, and Google Cloud Logging to track changes across your identity services. Combine these logs with event data from synchronization tools to spot patterns.

Monitor for Role Misuse and Escalation

Configure alerts for privilege escalation, including the creation of new admin accounts, changes to service principals, or deployment of machines tied to privileged identity roles.

Compare Baseline Machine Profiles

Use VM baselines to detect when new instances mimic the behavior or configuration of legitimate domain controllers or identity management nodes.

Network Traffic and Replication Analysis

Monitor internal traffic flows for replication-like behavior, including DNS registrations, Kerberos traffic, or unexpected API usage tied to authentication protocols.

5. Cloud-Native Prevention Tactics

Enforce Just-In-Time Privilege Access

Use tools like Azure Privileged Identity Management (PIM) to require approvals and expiration on admin access for identity services.

Implement Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) Boundaries

Ensure identity-related roles are split between multiple administrative tiers to reduce blast radius in case of compromise.

Secure Identity Synchronization Channels

Encrypt and authenticate traffic between on-prem and cloud identity bridges. Audit changes to sync configurations regularly.

Harden VM and Instance Creation Policies

Apply resource policies and tags to control and monitor where and how virtual machines—especially those hosting identity services—are created.

6. Response and Containment

Isolation and Network Quarantine

Immediately isolate suspected rogue infrastructure by disabling network access and suspending related cloud roles.

Analyze logs from the moment of suspicious activity to understand the attacker’s path and identify what roles or machines were accessed.

Using automated backup tools, restore identity configurations from a previously verified state. Roll back unauthorized VM deployments and revoke temporary privileges.

Conclusion: A Modern Defense for Modern Identity Systems

Traditional perimeter-based security is no longer enough. As identity becomes the new security boundary, the risks of unauthorized or simulated infrastructure grow. Detecting a —or any rogue identity component in the cloud—requires visibility, segmentation, and continuous verification.

Organizations that integrate logging, privilege controls, and cloud-native monitoring can stop unauthorized identity infrastructure before it causes widespread damage. Securing your identity backbone is not just a best practice—it’s essential for modern cloud resilience.

items.title: iOS Picture in Picture (PiP) Implementation Guide
items.author: Sylar
items.content:
Picture-in-Picture (PiP) is a feature that allows users to continue watching video content while using other applications. This guide provides a comprehensive walkthrough of implementing PiP functionality in iOS applications, including custom content rendering and system control management.

  • ✅ Core PiP interface implementation (setup, start, stop, release)
  • ✅ Custom content rendering with plugin separation
  • ✅ PiP window control style management
  • ✅ Automatic background PiP mode activation
  • ✅ PiP window size and aspect ratio adjustment
  • ✅ Custom content rendering demo (UIView image loop)
  • ⏳ Playback event monitoring and resource optimization
  • ⏳ Automatic implementation switching based on system version and app type
  • ⏳ MPNowPlayingSession integration for playback information
  • ⏳ Performance optimization and best practices

While Apple's official documentation primarily covers AVPlayer-based PiP implementation and VOIP PiP, it lacks detailed information about advanced features like custom rendering and control styles. This guide provides a complete implementation solution based on practical experience.

  1. The core implementation involves inserting a UIView (AVSampleBufferDisplayLayer) into the specified contentSourceView and rendering a transparent image. This approach enables PiP functionality without affecting the original content.

  2. Instead of using the standard video frame display method, we implement custom content rendering by dynamically adding a UIView to the PiP window. This approach offers greater flexibility and better encapsulation.

  1. Audio Session Configuration

    Even for videos without audio, setting the audio session to movie playback is essential. Without this configuration, the PiP window won't open when the app moves to the background.

  2. While controls fast-forward/rewind buttons, other controls (play/pause buttons, progress bar) require KVO-based configuration.

  3. Direct access to the PiP window's ViewController is not available. Two current implementation approaches:

    • Add views to the current active window
    • Access the Controller's private viewController property through reflection

2. Configure PiP Controller

3. Configure Control Style

4. Implement Playback Delegate

Your support is appreciated!

items.title: 7 Essential Web Accessibility Testing Strategies for Inclusive Development
items.author: Aarav Joshi
items.content:
As a best-selling author, I invite you to explore my books on Amazon. Don't forget to follow me on Medium and show your support. Thank you! Your support means the world!

Accessibility testing has become a critical component of web development, ensuring digital products are usable by everyone regardless of their abilities. In my years working with development teams, I've found that implementing structured accessibility testing workflows not only helps meet compliance requirements but also creates better experiences for all users.

Creating effective web accessibility testing is about establishing systematic approaches that identify barriers before they reach production. These workflows must balance automated and manual testing while considering the diverse needs of users with disabilities.

Web accessibility is fundamentally about equity. When we build accessible websites, we're recognizing that all users deserve equal access to information and functionality. This isn't just about compliance—it's about building products that work for everyone.

Automated accessibility testing offers efficiency and consistency in identifying common issues. I've integrated accessibility testing into CI/CD pipelines to catch problems early in the development process. This approach prevents accessibility debt from accumulating and becoming more costly to fix later.

While automated tools are valuable, they typically catch only about 30-40% of accessibility issues. The remaining problems require human judgment and testing. This is why comprehensive workflows must include both automated and manual approaches.

Keyboard testing is essential since many users with disabilities rely on keyboard navigation instead of a mouse. When conducting keyboard testing, I focus on verifying that all interactive elements are reachable and operable using only the keyboard, with clear visual focus indicators.

Screen readers are vital tools for users with visual impairments. Testing with screen readers helps verify that all content is properly announced and that the semantic structure makes sense aurally. I regularly test with popular screen readers like NVDA on Windows, VoiceOver on macOS, and JAWS to ensure compatibility across different assistive technologies.

To conduct effective screen reader testing, I focus on verifying that headings are properly nested, images have appropriate alt text, form fields have associated labels, and ARIA attributes are correctly implemented. This ensures that screen reader users can navigate and understand the content effectively.

Main Page TitleSection HeadingSome content with an  included.Subsection Heading

Color contrast is another critical aspect of accessibility. Ensuring sufficient contrast between text and background colors helps users with low vision, color blindness, or those using devices in bright sunlight. I use contrast analyzers to verify that text meets WCAG AA standards (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text).

Creating accessibility personas has transformed how my teams approach testing. By developing detailed profiles of users with different disabilities, we gain empathy and understand specific challenges these users face. For example, a persona named "Michael" might represent a user with motor impairments who navigates exclusively with a keyboard, while "Sarah" might represent a screen reader user with complete vision loss.

When testing with these personas in mind, we consider questions like: "How would Michael navigate this form without a mouse?" or "Would Sarah understand the context of this notification?" This approach helps identify issues that might otherwise be overlooked.

Component-level accessibility testing catches issues early in the development process. By testing individual components before they're integrated into pages, teams can address accessibility concerns when they're easiest to fix. This approach also promotes reusable, accessible components rather than fixing the same issues repeatedly.

Regular manual audits remain essential despite advances in automated testing. I schedule comprehensive reviews at key development milestones to identify issues that automated tools miss. These audits focus on complex interactions, contextual understanding, and subjective judgments about usability for people with disabilities.

A typical manual audit includes testing with keyboard navigation, screen readers, zoomed interfaces, and under various constraints that simulate different disabilities. The goal is to experience the site as users with disabilities would and identify barriers they might encounter.

User testing with people with disabilities provides invaluable insights that no other testing method can match. I've found that recruiting participants with various disabilities to test products reveals issues that even experienced accessibility professionals might miss. These sessions provide authentic feedback about real-world usage patterns and challenges.

When conducting user testing sessions with disabled participants, I focus on observing their natural interaction patterns rather than directing them too specifically. This reveals unexpected accessibility issues and workarounds that users have developed to navigate inaccessible content.

Implementing these strategies creates a comprehensive approach to accessibility testing. However, the process requires continuous refinement. I recommend documenting accessibility issues in detail, including steps to reproduce, the impact on users, and potential solutions.

 Dropdown menu not keyboard accessible
 Users who rely on keyboard navigation cannot access dropdown content
 2.1.1 Keyboard (Level A)
 Navigate to the navigation bar using Tab key
 Press Tab to focus on dropdown trigger
 Press Enter/Space - menu does not open


Add keyboard event handlers to toggle the dropdown:

javascript

dropdownButton.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {

if (e.key === 'Enter' || e.key === ' ') { e.preventDefault(); });

Testing for accessibility across different browsers and devices adds another layer of complexity. Browser inconsistencies in implementing accessibility APIs can cause variations in how assistive technologies interpret content. I regularly test across Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge, plus mobile browsers on iOS and Android.

Responsive design testing must consider accessibility implications. When layouts change at different viewport sizes, it's important to verify that all content remains accessible and that the reading order makes sense. This is particularly important for complex layouts that reorder content at different breakpoints.

Form accessibility deserves special attention in testing workflows. Complex forms often present significant barriers to users with disabilities. I verify that all form fields have proper labels, error messages are announced by screen readers, and validation doesn't rely solely on visual cues.

Email addressWe'll never share your email.Password
      Password must be at least 8 characters
    Submit

Custom interactive components require rigorous testing because they often lack built-in accessibility features. When implementing components like custom dropdowns, carousels, or modal dialogs, I test extensively with keyboard navigation and screen readers to ensure they follow appropriate ARIA patterns.

Automated accessibility testing tools have limitations. While tools like axe-core, WAVE, and Lighthouse can identify many technical violations, they cannot fully assess the usability of a site for people with disabilities. Understanding these limitations helps teams avoid a false sense of security from passing automated tests alone.

Documentation plays a crucial role in sustainable accessibility testing workflows. I maintain detailed accessibility guidelines, testing checklists, and remediation patterns that teams can reference. This documentation includes examples of both inaccessible and accessible implementations to illustrate best practices.

Establishing clear accessibility acceptance criteria before development begins helps teams understand requirements upfront. For each feature, I define specific accessibility requirements that must be met before it can be considered complete. This "shift-left" approach prevents accessibility from becoming an afterthought.

Feature: Product Search
Accessibility Acceptance Criteria:
- Search input must have visible label and be keyboard accessible
- Autocomplete suggestions must be navigable by keyboard
- Search results must announce count to screen readers
- Filtering options must be operable by keyboard and properly labeled
- Results must maintain focus position when sorted or filtered

Training teams on accessibility fundamentals improves testing effectiveness. When developers and QA specialists understand why certain patterns are problematic, they become better at identifying issues. I regularly conduct workshops covering WCAG principles, assistive technology basics, and common accessibility patterns.

The final strategy I recommend is conducting end-to-end accessibility testing. This approach tests complete user journeys rather than isolated features. By mapping out common paths through the application and testing them from start to finish, teams can identify issues that might arise from the interaction between components.

Implementing these strategies creates robust workflows that catch a wide range of accessibility issues. However, accessibility testing is not a one-time effort but an ongoing process. Technologies, standards, and user needs evolve, requiring continuous refinement of testing approaches.

By incorporating these comprehensive testing strategies, development teams can build more inclusive web experiences that work for all users, regardless of their abilities or how they access digital content.

items.title: Overthinking Kills Progress: Why You Need to Just Start
items.author: Chinomnso Ugba
items.content:
If you’ve been stuck in your head waiting for the “right time” to start something, this is your sign to stop overthinking and begin — even if it's messy.

"Start before you're ready. Don’t prepare, begin." — Mel Robbins

Overthinking is probably the sneakiest form of self-sabotage we don’t talk about enough. It disguises itself as planning. It makes you feel smart. It makes you feel like you're being careful. But really, it’s fear. Dressed up in a clean outfit and overused Google Docs.

And if you’re anything like me, you’ve probably wasted hours — or months — stuck in your own head instead of just making a move.

The Myth of the “Perfect Start”

We romanticize beginnings too much. We think the stars need to align. We think we need the best gear, the right mindset, a better laptop, or more confidence. But here’s the truth:

You don’t need clarity to begin. Clarity comes beginning.

Want to start writing? Write.

Want to start coding? Open a blank file.

Want to start freelancing? Send a message.

It will feel messy. You’ll cringe at your early work. But that’s how it’s supposed to be. That’s how progress happens.

The Trap of Research and “Readiness”

Don’t get me wrong, researching is important. But if you're constantly stuck in “just one more video,” or “let me finish this course first,” then it’s no longer research — it’s procrastination in disguise.

If you find yourself re-reading the same blog posts, hoarding bookmarks, and feeling more overwhelmed than informed, that’s your sign to stop collecting and start creating.

What Actually Builds Momentum?

  • Even tiny action. Especially tiny action.

    One blog post. One design. One pitch.

  • It doesn’t need to be daily. Just often enough that your brain stops treating it like a mountain.

  • You’ll mess up. You’ll waste time. You’ll cringe at your first attempts. But it’s fine. That’s data, not failure.

I’ve realized that overthinking isn’t just about fear — it’s also about control. We want to predict every outcome before we move. But life doesn’t work that way. Execution beats perfection. Every. Single. Time.

So here’s your reminder:Start that thing. Even if it’s awkward. Even if you feel unqualified. Start before you're ready.

That’s how everything worth doing begins.

If you found this post helpful, please leave a comment.

Share what you’re starting today — no matter how small.

Let’s make progress, not excuses.

items.title: 🚀 Meet WesBite AI — A Chatbot with Personality, Built Without Any AI APIs 🤖💬
items.author: WesBite_AI
items.content:
here is our AI WesBite just know this is only in Demo So its not very smart yet

This is where you can find our Hey

I'm excited to introduce WesBite AI, a project I've been building with nothing but HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. No backend, no external AI models, no servers — just good old browser-based code and a big idea:

What if an AI assistant could live in your browser, chat with you like a buddy, and actually feel like it had a personality?

WesBite AI is my attempt to bring that vision to life.

💡 What Is WesBite AI? WesBite AI is a chatbot with charm. It's like your browser’s built-in buddy — ready to answer questions, make small talk, and bring a little spark of conversation to your screen.

But here’s the twist: it’s all built from scratch. No OpenAI APIs, no fancy machine learning libraries. Just:

🧱 HTML to structure the chatbot interface

🎨 CSS to style it and give it character

🧠 JavaScript to power the brain and dialogue engine

Yup, it's a frontend-only, personality-packed chatbot made the old-school way.

🔧 How WesBite Works Here’s the behind-the-scenes breakdown of how this charming AI operates:

🗂️ 1. Static Files, Dynamic Chat The entire project runs on the client side — meaning once you load the page, everything runs in the browser with no need for server calls. This makes WesBite:

🧠 2. Pattern-Based Dialogue WesBite uses a combination of keyword matching and pattern recognition. Here's the secret sauce:

If you say “hello,” it knows how to greet you back.

Ask it “Do you know ChatGPT" It will respond with "ChatGPT is a good friend of mine and I am hoping to someday be as popular as ChatGPT"

It has fallback responses when it doesn’t understand something — and even those have personality!

Think of it like a friendly script that adapts based on what you say. It's not true AI (yet), but it sure tries to feel like it.

🎭 3. Personality Matters One of WesBite’s core goals is to feel like you're chatting with a person, not a robot. So the responses are:

Sometimes a little quirky

And always open to improvement

🌱 Future Plans for WesBite This is just the beginning. Here’s what I’m planning next:

✅ Smarter Conversations Right now, WesBite uses pattern-matching. I want to evolve that into something closer to natural language understanding, maybe even training a lightweight model locally or integrating smarter decision trees.

🎨 Theming & Customization Let users choose between chatbot “personalities” — maybe a funny WesBite, a super helpful WesBite, or even a grumpy WesBite?

🌐 Deploy It Properly Right now WesBite is still local, but I’m working on hosting it on GitHub Pages or Replit, and then sharing it with the world so anyone can try it out.

🧠 Learn Mode In the long term, I want WesBite to have a “learn” mode where you can teach it new responses and behaviors through a simple UI.

🤔 Why Build It Without AI APIs? Honestly? A few reasons:

Creative challenge — I wanted to see how far I could go with just front-end code.

School laptop struggles — My computer has tons of things blocked, so I had to work within those limits.

Ownership — WesBite is entirely Lucas Studios. Every line of code, every line of dialogue.

💬 Sample Chat with WesBite You: Do you know WesBite?

WesBite AI: Oh yeah! WesBite is a good friend of mine. Not as smart as me (yet), but learning fast!

You: What can you do? WesBite AI: I can chat, share random thoughts, and try to make your day just a little weirder. Ready?

You: Tell me a joke. WesBite AI: Why don't scientists trust atoms? Because they make up everything 😏

🎉 Try It Out Soon! WesBite will be going live soon on GitHub Pages or another platform, and I’ll post the link right here when it’s ready. Until then, if you’re curious about the code, want to collaborate, or just want to say hi — drop a comment!

🧑‍💻 Final Thoughts WesBite AI isn’t just a project — it’s my playground for learning, building, and experimenting with the boundaries of what a “simple” web chatbot can do. It’s full of heart, code, and caffeine ☕.

If you’re building something weird, fun, or challenging like this — keep going. The internet needs more wild little projects like WesBite.

Thanks for reading! 🫶 — The Creator of WesBite

items.title: Non-Access Modifiers in Java
items.author: Sudhakar V
items.content:
Non-access modifiers in Java control various aspects of classes, methods, and variables beyond just their visibility. Unlike access modifiers (public, private, protected), these modifiers don't control access levels but instead provide other functionality.

Main Non-Access Modifiers

  • Makes a member belong to the class rather than instances
  • Can be applied to:
    • Variables (class variables)
  • Makes elements unchangeable after initialization
  • Can be applied to:
    • Classes (cannot be extended)
    • Methods (cannot be overridden)
    • Variables (cannot be reassigned)
  • Used for incomplete implementations
  • Can be applied to:
    • Classes (cannot be instantiated)
    • Methods (no body, must be implemented by subclasses)
  • Controls thread access to methods/blocks
  • Ensures only one thread can execute at a time
  • Marks variables not to be serialized
  • Used with object serialization
  • Ensures variable is always read from/written to main memory
  • Used in multithreading environments
  • Indicates a method is implemented in platform-dependent code (like C)
  • Used with Java Native Interface (JNI)
  • Ensures consistent floating-point calculations across platforms
  • Can be applied to classes, interfaces, and methods
Variables, methods, nested classes, blocksClass-level rather than instance-level
Classes, methods, variablesPrevent modification/extension
Incomplete implementations
Exclude from serialization
Visibility across threads
Platform-native implementation
Classes, interfaces, methodsConsistent floating-point

  1. Some modifiers cannot be combined:

    • + (contradictory)
    • + (private methods can't be overridden)
    • + (static methods can't be overridden)

  2. Method-specific modifiers:

    • , , are only for methods

  3. Variable-specific modifiers:

    • , are only for variables

Understanding these non-access modifiers is essential for writing correct, efficient, and maintainable Java code that properly leverages language features.

items.title: What's in a List
items.author: José Ramón (JR)
items.content:
is an interface in Java Collections that represents an ordered sequence of elements.

  • Access elements by position (index).
  • Store duplicate elements, because a Set does not store duplicates.
  • Insert or remove elements at any position.

This is the canonical definition of a List in Java.

The most common implementation

List being an interface, the most used implementation is the class:

we can access elements by index:

It is also possible to remove elements by index:

It is quite simple. It is like an array, but we do not have to worry about resizing it. The class takes care of that. The notation is also different. We use instead of to access elements by index.

Most of the methods are self-explanatory. The main ones are:

  • : adds an element to the end of the list.
  • : removes an element from the list.
  • : returns the element at the specified index.
  • : returns the number of elements in the list.
  • : returns if the list is empty, otherwise.
  • : returns if the list contains the specified element, otherwise.
  • : returns the index of the first occurrence of the specified element in the list, or if the element is not found.
  • : returns the index of the last occurrence of the specified element in the list, or if the element is not found.

Two methods are going to be explained, because they are a bit confusing.

is an overloaded method. It has two versions:

The first version adds the element to the end of the list, while the second version adds the element at the specified index. The second one would have rather been called .

set(int index, E element) replaces the element at the specified index with the given element. It return the element that was previously at the specified position.

  • The list must have an element at the specified index, otherwise it throws an exception.
  • It changes the element in place, so it does not change the size of the list.

Why not use the implementation always?

The class is a good choice for most cases, in fact most of the programmers have only used that class when creating a List.

But there is one thing that the class does not do very well: inserting and removing elements in random list positions. Why? Because every time an element is inserted or removed, the remaining elements starting from the modified position have to be shifted, since all the array elements must be stored in contiguous memory.

As a consequence, an should be faster than an for adding or removing elements in the middle of the list. Unless you have to use a cache memory, in which case you will prefer a list whose elements are contiguous in memory.

Since both classes implement the interface, we can use them interchangeably.

Let's see how a simplified looks like:

As we can see, it is a doubly linked list. A node has three attributes: the data itself, and references to the next and previous nodes.

These nodes are not contiguous in memory. Therefore, when an element is inserted or removed, the remaining elements do not have to be shifted. A node has to modify only its two references, resulting in much faster operations.

+-------+ +-------+ +-------+ | 10 |-->--| 20 |-->--| 30 |

+-------+ +-------+ +-------+

Node A Node B Node C

+-------+ +-------+ | 10 |-->--| 30 | Node A Node C

The class is faster for adding and removing elements in the middle of the list, while the class is faster for adding and removing elements at the beginning or end of the list.

Let's see an example for measuring the time it takes to add and remove elements in the middle of the list:

If we run this program, it turns out that the insertion time is much greater than the one, although all the insertions are done in the middle of the list.

It seems that the time it takes to traverse the list from head/tail to reach a middle index is higher for the . This happens because the data structure is optimized for shifting elements by using ; whereas traversing all the nodes is slow because nodes are scattered in memory. Each pointer access can result in a memory , which makes the process even slower. In other words, insertion in a LinkedList is a operation.

copies memory blocks from one part of an array to another. It's a , that is, its core logic is implemented in a lower-level language like C (or C++) for performance reasons. If a traditional loop were used to copy every single list element, the traversing time would be much higher.

ArrayList is faster due to contiguous memory and optimized block copying, not because it's a faster algorithm in terms of complexity.

The benchmark highlights that algorithmic complexity alone does not determine real-world performance. ArrayList’s optimized memory operations make it a strong contender even for middle insertions. We should profile our specific use case to make the most informed decision.

The missing piece: ListIterator

The class is faster for adding and removing elements at the middle of the list.

but the results obtained in the benchmark did not match that statement.

We can use a and move it to a position and then do many insertions/removals at that spot. In this way, we don't need to traverse the list each time.

The insertions time for the is very close to zero, and it's much faster than the ones for the .

insertion and removals are faster when using a , provided an iterator at the correct position is used. In these cases, insertion in a LinkedList is a operation.

A is suitable for use cases where we need to add and remove elements at a known same position we don't need to access elements by index often. Examples are undo/redo stacks and managing history.

implements both the and interfaces, which means that it is a good choice for use cases where we need to add and remove elements from both ends of the list.

The Java is an interface with methods for adding, removing, and accessing elements. It has two different implementations: ArrayList and LinkedList.

offers excellent general-purpose performance, especially for random access and adding/removing at the end. provides faster insertion and deletion if an at the correct position is used. As always, the optimal choice depends on your application's specific needs and access patterns.

items.title: How Tariffs Are Impacting Bitcoin Miners and What It Means for QA
items.author: Philip Laurens
items.content:
As a , it’s impossible to ignore the far-reaching effects of recent tariff hikes on Bitcoin mining operations. Tariffs—especially the 10% duty on Chinese imports announced in —have disrupted the global supply chain, with mining hardware being one of the most affected sectors.

In this blog, I’ll reflect on how these tariff impacts have shifted the way we test and validate mining hardware and software.

The Tariff Effect on Bitcoin Mining Hardware 💸

U.S. miners are facing multiple challenges due to these tariffs. The price of , especially those manufactured in , has skyrocketed. The added on top of existing duties (25% from 2018) is making equipment more expensive and, in some cases, . This not only affects miners’ profitability but also directly impacts QA teams like ours, who rely on for testing and validation.

As a result, we’ve had to rethink how we approach . With thousands of mining rigs held up in customs or delayed in transit, there’s a growing need for in our QA processes.

The Shift to Software Testing and Virtual Environments 🖥️

In the face of shipping delays, hardware testing has become a . We can’t always get our hands on the latest rigs for validation purposes, so we’ve had to pivot toward and virtual testing environments. These alternative methods help us simulate hardware performance under various conditions, allowing us to maintain our testing cadence.

While not a perfect substitute for real-world hardware, these enable us to ensure that our is ready to work with whatever hardware comes our way—even if it’s delayed or suboptimal.

Optimizing Software for Hardware Limitations ⚙️

As a result of hardware availability issues, the QA team is placing a stronger focus on . We know that miners will have to rely on or less powerful rigs for the time being. As part of our QA strategy, we’ve started testing our software to ensure it can run efficiently on both the latest high-end and older mining rigs that may become more prevalent due to the .

The key here is to ensure that the software can handle varying levels of power, processing capabilities, and network latency while maintaining and . It’s a shift from just validating for high-end hardware to ensuring and across diverse systems.

Adjusting Testing Protocols to Evolving Hardware Needs 🔄

Given the unpredictable nature of hardware availability, our QA testing protocols have evolved. Instead of focusing solely on testing with the latest models, we are expanding our scope to cover a of hardware configurations. This means verifying compatibility with a variety of , some of which may be older, less efficient, or lower in demand due to .

This shift ensures that when new rigs finally arrive, we won’t be caught off guard. It’s about building into the QA process so that our systems can function seamlessly, no matter what hardware is available in the market.

Looking Forward: Flexibility is Key 🔑

The ongoing tariff situation has underscored an essential lesson in quality assurance: is crucial. The ability to adapt our testing methods to meet changing circumstances is a . By focusing on and ensuring compatibility across multiple hardware types, we can guarantee the stability and reliability of our mining operations—regardless of the state of the hardware supply chain.

In conclusion, while the tariff-induced delays and price hikes are a headache for miners, they also present an opportunity for QA professionals to rethink how they approach their work. By being proactive, embracing virtual testing, and focusing on , we can continue to ensure that mining operations are as and as possible, no matter the challenges thrown our way.

items.title: JAVA - POLYMORPHISM
items.author: Sudhakar V
items.content:
Polymorphism is one of the four fundamental principles of object-oriented programming (OOP) in Java, along with encapsulation, inheritance, and abstraction. It allows objects to take on many forms and enables flexible, reusable code.

Types of Polymorphism in Java

1. Compile-Time Polymorphism (Static Polymorphism)

: Multiple methods with the same name but different parameters in the same class.

  • Determined at compile time
  • Based on method signature (name + parameters)
  • Return type alone doesn't differentiate overloaded methods

2. Runtime Polymorphism (Dynamic Polymorphism)

: Subclass provides specific implementation of a method already defined in its superclass.

  • Uses method overriding with same signature as parent class
  • Annotated with (recommended)

Key Concepts in Polymorphism

Creating a parent class reference variable for child class objects.

Converting a parent class reference back to child class type.

Used to check the actual type of an object before downcasting.

Polymorphism with Interfaces

Java interfaces provide another powerful way to implement polymorphism:

  1. : Write more generic and reusable code
  2. : Easily extend and modify existing code
  3. : Reduce code complexity and improve organization
  4. : Add new functionality without modifying existing code
  5. Interface-based Programming: Work with abstractions rather than concrete implementations

Polymorphism Rules in Java

  1. Method overriding cannot reduce visibility (can't override public with private)
  2. Final methods cannot be overridden
  3. Static methods cannot be overridden (method hiding instead)
  4. Private methods are not polymorphic (not visible to subclasses)
  5. Constructors cannot be polymorphic

Understanding polymorphism is essential for writing flexible, maintainable Java applications that leverage object-oriented principles effectively.

items.title: What is a CCNA Certificate?
items.author: Skill Tester Techy
items.content:
Having spent over 10 years in IT training, cloud technologies, and helping professionals upskill, one of the most common questions I get from new learners is:—and even more when paired with platforms like .

Let’s dive into the essentials and why the CCNA and cloud skills are a killer combo in 2025. 🚀

“A strong foundation builds skyscrapers. In tech, that foundation is networking.” 🧠

📘 What Is a CCNA Certificate?

The CCNA—which stands for Cisco Certified Network Associate—is an entry-level information technology (IT) certification issued by networking hardware company Cisco.

This globally recognized credential is designed to validate your knowledge on fundamental networking concepts, including:

  • IP addressing & subnetting
  • Routing and switching basics
  • Network access & connectivity
  • Automation and programmability

Whether you're aiming to become a network admin, cloud engineer, or even a cybersecurity analyst, CCNA lays the groundwork.

🤖 Why CCNA Matters Even in the Cloud Era (Especially with Google Cloud!)

While many workloads have moved to the cloud, understanding core networking is more crucial than ever. Why?

Because cloud platforms like still run on virtual networks, routing protocols, firewalls, and secure access. If you understand how traditional networking works (CCNA-level skills), transitioning into (like VPCs, Cloud NAT, and Private Google Access) becomes so much easier.

And you’ll see how closely the concepts align with what CCNA teaches.

“Networking is the invisible force that makes the cloud work. Master it, and you master the cloud.” ☁️🔗

🔍 What You'll Learn in a CCNA Course

Here’s a quick peek at what you’ll tackle when preparing for the CCNA:

  • Network models (OSI & TCP/IP)
  • Ethernet and switching technologies
  • IPv4 & IPv6 configuration
  • Routing protocols (like OSPF)
  • Basic firewall and security setups
  • Intro to SDN (Software Defined Networking)

🚀 Who Should Get CCNA Certified?

  • College grads or tech beginners looking to break into IT
  • wanting to pivot into networking or cloud
  • Cloud practitioners on Google Cloud, AWS, or Azure who want to strengthen their networking foundation
  • dealing with hybrid networks and security

🔗 Helpful Blogs & Learning Resources

The CCNA certificate is more than just a line on your resume—it’s a confidence boost, a career opener, and a stepping stone into cloud-native roles. If you're aiming for Google Cloud, hybrid infrastructure, or advanced security roles, CCNA gives you the strong base you need.

“The cloud is the future, but networking is the path. Walk it with CCNA.” 🌉

So go ahead—take the leap, get CCNA certified, and start your journey toward mastering cloud technologies like . 🧑‍💻✨
